sentences.text: Some people came to visit us, because they had family in Europe, you know-- different cities, you know, in Europe and Poland.
So they came see-- to ask us, maybe did you find-- maybe did you see a brother of mine, a sister of mine, or who else?
But we didn't know the people.
But they were very nice to us.
They brought us over to the Jewish Federation, and they start doing something for us, you know?
So they gave us-- after that, they made in the same building-- they send us in a-- a woman.
She was the manager.
And they made-- opened up a kitchen for us there.
She was the cook.
She was cooking for us.
And she was preparing food.
Then we start already-- living already.
It was already a bit better than in-- in camp, in Germany.
So then the group-- from the group-- from this group-- the guys, you know, the boys over there, you know, already 25, 26, 30 people-- all 30 years old, you know-- older than that.
You know, they start buying cigarettes in Belgium and going back to Germany to sell over there.
Because they didn't have nothing in Germany at that time.
So one day, I said to mine-- to one of-- of the guys, I says, well, you're going to Germany.
I'll give you a picture of mine.
Put down my name.
Put down where I'm-- where I'm-- where I have.
And I said, if you go to Germany, if you see somebody, ask-- show them the picture.
Maybe they know somebody-- a sister of mine, or a brother of mine.
You never know.
Matter of fact, that he came to one of the camps-- in the front of the camp.
It was in Zeilsheim.
One of the camp-- in front of the camp was a policeman.
It was already after the liberation.
But people still lived in the camps at that time, because they didn't know where to go.
From there, everybody, you know, made his way to another country and wherever.
While a lot of people remained in Germany too.
So I gave him a picture of mine.
He says-- and this policeman said to me-- it was a landsman of mine from the same hometown.
He says, sure I know.
Her sister is alive, and she's in another camp.
And I have her picture too.
My sister did the same thing like I did.
She wrote on the other-- on the other side of the picture her name.
And by that time, it was quite a few months already.
By that time, she was married already at that time after the liberation-- after the war.
So-- and he-- he left them my picture.
And he-- to him-- and he said, you give this picture to her sister if you see her.
And he took the picture from him.
He gave it to me.
He brought it to me.
One day, I was in the-- in the room.
I heard somebody was walking the stairs up, and he said-- two guys came over.
And he says-- one to the next one-- but don't tell her nothing.
Just we have to prepare her slowly so she shouldn't pass out.
Matter of fact, they knock my door.
I opened the door.
And they came in.
And my heart was pumping like a-- what can I tell you?
And I was afraid that they're going to bring me bad news.
So they said, sit down.
And they sat down.
And that's-- we will have to talk with you.
So they prepared me.
I shouldn't get excited at the moment.
They told me that, I brought you a picture of your sister.
And I passed out right there.
So they revived me.
And I sat down.
And I looked at the other side of the picture.
She wrote me that she's married.
She's here and here.
And you can imagine how excited it was-- what kind of excited it was for me.
And I knew that she's there, that she's alive.
I figured, well, if she's alive, it's good.
Then I can already correspond with her.
And that's what happened.
The next day, I wrote right away a letter to her.
And she wrote me a letter that my brother is alive too-- the younger one from-- my sister is the oldest, and I am the middle one-- and my brother, younger brother.
My brother came to Italy with aliyah youngsters.
He was 9, 10 years old at that time.
He came to Italy, and they sent them from there to Israel.
Those-- those kids, they'd sent right away, direct from Italy to Israel.
And I got this news from my sister.
But my sister wrote to me.
Don't come.
I-- as much as I want to see you, I don't want you to come back to Germany.
Because I wrote her a letter.
It was probably 10-- 10 pages.
And she said, don't come to Germany, because if you left already, it doesn't make sense to come back.
And we're not going to stay either-- either here.
Because we're going to leave pretty soon.
So I-- we were just corresponding.
Wherever-- she had my address already.
And wherever she went, she was-- she was writing me letters.
He brought back then.
I figure like this.
I was there in Belgium.
And I start working a little bit there, helping out over there with that woman who was the manager in the kitchen.
I went to her, you know, making the shopping for the-- for us, for the food, and everything.
Whatever I could, I did for her.
And I got paid from the Jewish Federation.
They didn't pay much, but at least they gave me just pocket money.
And this pocket money, I saved up every penny, because I didn't have to go buy food.
I ate in the kitchen-- so in the restaurant-- what we had downstairs.
So I saved up all the money, and I buy for myself clothes.
I was, at that time, when I came out from camp, I was 19 going on 20th-- still unexperienced.
What should I do now?
I didn't know-- no trade.
They-- they interrupt our school.
I had seven grades only-- interrupt our school.
What can I go do now?
But I figured, I am free now.
Whatever I'm going to do, it doesn't make any difference.
The main thing, I'm free.
I have my two hands.
I feel OK.
I feel fine.
Now we start eating.
I got too-- too chubby at that time already.
Because, [LAUGHS] everything-- yeah.
We-- this was what we didn't have during the war, so we made it up after the war.
And we lived there in Belgium-- practically 9 to 10-- until 10 months.
We used to go out to the movies.
We used to be already with people there.
We used to dress nice.
One day, mine husband-- he was not my husband.
He was just a friend-- he was with three guys in a room.
He was sick.
One came home-- came down to the restaurant and says, well, Nathan is sick, and he's in bed with 39 temperature.
Maybe you would bring over a-- a cup of tea with lemon?
I said, fine.
I pour a cup of tea there.
And before then, his friend-- he had a girlfriend.
And she registered herself to go to Israel.
She had family there.
My-- my husband had a father there too.
That's why he wanted to go to Israel so badly.
He was very anxious to go there.
She registered to go to Israel, but they took her-- the first group-- from Belgium.
She went, the first one, with a legal aliyah, with a beautiful passenger ship.
And mine-- and then when I went up to give my-- mine husband-- to bring over the tea for him, I see-- I knock at the door, nobody answers.
I said, what's the matter?
Somebody came down and said that he is so sick with temperature, and all of a sudden, he's not there?
So I figured, maybe he's sound asleep.
Maybe I'll go back later.
I came down, pour out the tea, made him another cup of tea.
And I went up again after an hour.
Nobody answered again.
I asked the guys, what's the matter?
Where is he?
You know what?
Today is aliyah going away from Marseille, from France.
And his friend-- his girlfriend-- went with this aliyah.
So he said that he's not going to stay there.
He went to Marseille, found over there an American soldier.
He said to him, I'm going to disappear from here.
I don't want to be anymore here.
I want to go to Israel.
I have a father there.
He gave him a uniform.
The American soldier gave him-- brought him a uniform with-- like an American soldier.
And he told him, I'll give you something, but you cannot go up on the ship, because you don't have any papers to go.
You're not legal on the ship.
He says, don't worry about me.
Give me just something I could be-- I should be able to go on the ship.
He gave him something to carry on the ship-- that's pretend that he is, you know, a worker, or he's helping the people to go on the ship.
He took something and brought it over on the ship.
And he hid himself there.
He was hiding himself all the 10 days until they arrived to-- to Haifa.
He didn't show nobody his face, because otherwise, they would send him back.
But this German soldier, he helped him.
He gave him food.
You should have for every day, and you should be able to-- to go there.
One day, before the-- the ship arrived to Haifa, he ran out of the ship.
And he went to his father.
But what I want to tell you is about myself, that we went.
And then, I said, now, my brother's in Israel.
What I'm going to do now here?
Everybody's trying to-- to go away on his own, you know.
One is going to France.
One is going to-- to Belgium.
I didn't want to stay, mainly because you didn't have a chance to be there.
Because in Belgium, nobody can stay there-- a foreigner-- only those who are natives-- native, you know, Belgian people.
But I got acquainted with a-- with a family in-- in Belgium.
All of a sudden, I got sick.
I got an attack [BACKGROUND NOISE] on an appendix.
Took me to the-- to the hospital.
I was operated.
And after the surgery, I got very chubby after that.
And I said, well, I'm not going to stay here.
My brother used to write me letters.
My brother find out from my sister that I'm in Belgium.
He wrote me letters.
And I said, I'm going to come as soon as I can.
Then, they noted-- they notified us from the Jewish Federation that they making an illegal aliyah to go to Israel.
I said, if I'm going to wait now until they're going to give me permission to go, a certificate to go to Israel, then I'm going to wait until I'm going to get grey, probably.
So I said, no.
I am going to register myself.
I'm young.
What do I afraid?
If I went through Hitler, [BACKGROUND NOISE] I'm not afraid anymore.
I'll go.
And I registered myself.
And I went with a illegal aliyah.
We've-- brought us to-- to-- to Marseille.
They put us on a ship.
The name of the ship was [NON-ENGLISH]-- a beautiful, luxurious ship.
And the whole group, it was-- they were-- they were registering people from all over Belgium, from different cities.
And they made aliyah from France, and from all over Europe.
And they brought us to Marseille, and from there, on the ship.
We came on the ship.
There-- over there, we got already some food.
They fed us already.
And everything was fine.
We drove-- we were probably on the ship, I would say, about four or five days.
To Israel, to go by ship, it took 12 days.
Five days, we were on the ship.
All of a sudden, we said, from far away, we notice another ship was circling around the ocean there.
And we didn't know what's going to happen.
So they came one morning, and they told us that we have to go down this ship and going to transfer on the other ship.
Because this ship is going to make another aliyah.
It's going to go back to Marseille.
You can imagine, in the middle of the ocean, five days being on the ocean, with those ladders going down into the small, you know, little boats.
Brought us to the other ship-- we came over there to the other ship wet-- soaking wet-- all of us.
And this-- the-- they transferred the whole ship to the other ship.
We came on the other ship up there.
I got-- it was dark in my eyes-- dirty, filthy ship.
It was not a passenger ship-- a transport ship, you know.
The ship was bringing to the other ships, probably, coals, woods, whatever they needed, you know.
But I said, never mind.
Five days, we were on the ship.
We're going to go on this ship another five days.
Somehow, we're going to make it.
We were on this ship for last six days-- no food, no water, nothing whatsoever.
We were drinking the water from the ocean, can you imagine?
It's just like somebody give you to eat herring and no water.
That's how we were there.
Finally, we came close to Haifa port.
The ship starts sinking.
So our captain was an Israeli from a kibbutz.
He asked for help from the English people.
So they said-- because this was at that time all English people, you know.
They said, go back from where you came.
We didn't send for you.
They didn't want to let us into Haifa.
Don't you think that they'll-- they came with open arms for us?
No, no way.
But somehow, we made it-- hardly.
We made it until Port Haifa.
The captain disappeared right away, because otherwise, they will kill him.
He disappeared.
And he says, kids, now you're on your own.
Wherever you want to go, you can go.
No, it was not like that.
They took us from there to a camp, to Atlit, in Haifa.
This was-- you know what Atlit is.
Did you hear about it?
We were there for a month, isolated from everybody.
No-- nobody could get close to us, because they were very much afraid of some diseases.
Because we were with five days without food, with no water, with nothing.
Tell us about Atlit-- for the people who don't know.
So we were in Atlit there a month.
We couldn't go out.
We were only there walking around in the camp.
At least we were free there.
They fed us.
They gave us food, and everything.
Because this was by the English times, don't forget.
One day, my brother knew already that I am in Atlit, in Haifa.
It was very bad there, very bad, the circumstances, you know.
We didn't have clean, you know, part where to sleep.
Wooden-- such a wooden-- you know.
We had mattresses.
On our mattresses, we slept.
But everything was good for us, because we went through worse than this.
So we were there a month, isolated from everybody.

One day, my brother, I don't know how, somehow, he found out in Israel-- he was in Jerusalem in a kibbutz where he worked four days a week-- I mean, four days-- four hours a day-- I'm sorry-- four hours a day.
And four hours a day, he worked.
So-- and his [NON-ENGLISH] was Weitzman's wife.
He was very much acquainted with her, my brother, because she pitied my brother.
He came with three-- two more friends, as children, to Israel, to that school.
And she pitied him.
And he came to her like he would come to a mother and tell her his-- tell her-- tell her his story.
He came over to her, and he says, my sister is now in Atlit.
And I would like to go see her.
She says, you cannot go, because they wouldn't let you.
They're isolated.
Maybe you're gonna-- you're gonna, you know, be-- get sick after that.
Because the people-- you don't know if the people are sick or if they healthy.
Nobody got sick.
Everybody was fine.
But the English people made us over there stay.
One day, I'm-- we're sitting on-- on-- we sitting in-- in that camp on the-- it was a bar, you know, in the camp outside.
All of a sudden, we see-- because the Atlit is around the ocean.
We see a little boat came closer there.
My brother said to his-- to his [NON-ENGLISH], I would like to go to Haifa.
She says, no, you cannot go.
He says, I'm going to go.
Give me just a few dollars-- a few, you know, some money.
I should be able to get there.
She gave him some money.
And made sure he came there.
But how he came there, nobody knows.
Nobody knew about it.
Because everybody said, you're crazy.
You're not going to get there.
He says, I'm going to get there.
He came to the Port, to Haifa.
And he asked that he wants to work on that ship, that he should be able to get closer to the ship where we were isolated at Atlit.
The ship was standing there.
The one we came with, the ship, was still standing in port there.
He thought that we are on the ship, but we were already in Atlit.
He came with a little boat.
And he was screaming, hello, hello, hello, everybody!
Somebody was standing outside.
He says, she is not here.
She's the next-- she's in the next barrack there.
So he came.
And he got up on the ship.
He got off the-- the boat.
And he came to me.
And he says-- he brought me some food at that time.
And he says, well, you're here.
That's the main thing.
Now I'm going to see what I can do for you.
He came back.
The next day, he came back to Haifa-- I mean, to Jerusalem, where we were-- he was in school.
And he said to his [NON-ENGLISH], you know I just came down from Haifa.
She says, no.
I don't believe you.
He says, yes, here.
I have a picture of my sister.
He brought the-- I gave him a picture of mine, so.
And she said to him, well, what are you going to do?
He worked over there with the men and lived in Jerusalem.
He was a builder.
He was building the kibbutz.
And every Friday night for Shabbat, he took one of those three guys home, make him feel home, like he used to be home, like by us.
Every Friday, he brought somebody else home.
So my brother was once there, and he said to him, look.
My sister is in Atlit.
She came from camp.
Where is she going to be here?
We don't have family in Israel.
We didn't have nobody there.
What am I going to do?
He said-- and he was really a poor man.
He had four children in the house-- working very hard in Israel in the hot days, you know, as a builder, outside, you know, on-- on the structure of the building.
So he says, you know what-- and his wife said the same thing-- we have four children.
We're going to have a fifth one.
My brother was overwhelmed, you know, when he heard this.
He says, well, it's just temporary, you know, just let her go out from Atlit.
And then we'll see what we're going to do.
Matter of fact, that I came out from-- from Atlit, I had some clothes with me.
I had a little suitcase with me and nice clothes I bought for myself.
I came with a bus to Jerusalem.
Coming off the bus, everybody's waiting to get their suitcases-- their little belongings, what they brought.
I'm waiting.
My suitcase is not here.
Everybody got the suitcases.
I was the lucky one that didn't get my suitcase.
Imagine.
The next day, I didn't have nothing to change.
I just had a little purse with me-- small things-- a toothbrush, a comb, a lipstick, something like that, but no big things.
I didn't have-- they stole my-- my suitcase.
And I had beautiful things then.
I was very heartbroken-- very heartbroken.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] I said-- so my brother says, what can you do?
This is not going to be the end of the world.
Clothes you can get.
The main thing is the life.
Then he went back again to his [NON-ENGLISH].
And he said to her, my sister is here.
They stole her suitcase.
She needs some clothes.
I have no money to buy her.
She says to him, don't worry about it.
I'll take care of her.
She loved him very much.
My brother's a little smarty, you know.
And she says, let her come next day, and I'll take her with me to Ata.
Remember Ata stores in Israel?
This was-- they had the most beautiful clothes there, and this lasts for ages.
You can never use up those-- those clothes.
She went with me into the store.
I went in.
I was very, very heartbroken, very ashamed, that I had to ask for clothes.
I didn't have no money.
I couldn't ask those people, because they were poor people themselves.
And they didn't have clothes for me.
So I went in with her to the store, and she bought for me clothes.
So I was already happy that I had something to change.
Then I lived with this family for three months.
I told them, look.
I'm here.
I'm young.
I want to have a job.
This family had a big family there in Israel, Jerusalem.
They worked all over.
One of the brothers was a milkman.
He came into the house.
And I said to him, maybe you'll be so kind and see that I should get a job.
I don't like to sit with my hands just like that, you know, I said, and-- and do nothing.
I'm young.
I have to start living now, because this is not my time.
This is my time now.
So he says-- the next day, he came.
He says, would you like to go and do work in a-- how do you call?
In a-- it's like a [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
In a-- how would I say this in English?
People are coming, you know, for-- for rest over there, you know.
And the food was outstanding there.
They cooked over there, and they-- it was like a [NON-ENGLISH], in Hebrew.
And I worked there quite a few months.
He get me-- he got me this job.
And I was so happy to have this job.
I worked over there with the cook, in the kitchen.
And matter of fact, I learned a lot of things from her at that time.
And she was cooking.
And I worked only a few hours a day the beginning.
They paid me very well.
I was very happy.
She gave me a lot of food home for the family.
So then I helped them already, which I was very, very, very happy about it.
And I worked some kind of three months there.
And I had friends in Tel Aviv.
Being in Jerusalem for three months, one of my friends sent me a letter, and she said, you came to Israel, and you're here only in Jerusalem.
You didn't see nothing yet.
I said, well, I have to start-- I'm working now, so I cannot go away.
But one day, my brother said to me, you know what?
You have to see Tel Aviv too.
So we took off one Saturday morning with the Arabic bus.
We got on the bus.
We came to Tel Aviv.
We stayed in Tel Aviv Saturday morning.
Saturday morning, we came to Tel Aviv.
We stayed all day with my friends.
And the next day, Sunday morning, we went home.
And so we went home morning.
I packed my suitcase.
I'm walking-- I'm walking with my brother in Tel Aviv, and-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the station, to the bus station.
All of a sudden, I see a truck pass by.
We were walking straight ahead.
And the other way, across to us, stopped a truck.
And my husband, my present husband, was in that truck.
He said to me, what are you doing here?
I says, well, I just came about maybe a few months ago.
I'm here a few months already.
Why didn't you let me know that you're here?
So how do I know your address?
I don't know where you are all together.
He came to his father.
And he said, you know what?
I'm going out with a girl, the sabra.
And maybe if I'm going to consider to get married, I will invite you for the wedding.
I says, fine.
Give me your address.
I gave him mine address.
And he wrote it down-- my address.
And I left.
And I said to my-- to him, bye.
We'll see you later!
